explicit_text: I grew up in Columbia, South Carolina, and I know the town well, especially the black neighborhoods around Main Street, Eighth Street, and Woodland Street. In 1946, I went back there after hearing about the racial troubles that erupted, which some called a race riot but I saw more as an invasion by the state into the black community. The courthouse was just a block away from where many black-owned businesses were located, and the tension between the black residents and white authorities was intense during that time.
implicit_text: I grew up in the capital city of South Carolina, and I know the town well, especially the black neighborhoods around Main Street, Eighth Street, and Woodland Street. In 1946, I went back there after hearing about the racial troubles that erupted, which some called a race riot but I saw more as an invasion by the state into the black community. The courthouse was just a block away from where many black-owned businesses were located, and the tension between the black residents and white authorities was intense during that time.

explicit_text: I served in World War II for about three or four years, fighting against the Japanese in the Pacific. The war was sold to us as a fight for American democracy and a better way of life compared to what leaders like Hitler, Tojo, and Mussolini were imposing. Coming back from the war, I expected to return to a country that lived up to those ideals, but the racial injustices I saw in Columbia, South Carolina, made me realize the struggle was far from over at home.

Coming back from the war, I expected to return to a country that lived up to those ideals, but the racial injustices I saw in the South Carolina capital made me realize the struggle was far from over at home.

explicit_text: During my time in World War II, I fought in the Guadalcanal campaign, which was one of the key battles against the Japanese in the Pacific. It was a tough fight, and I learned a lot about combat and survival there. The experience shaped how I handled myself later, especially when I had to defend my mother back home in Columbia after the war.

explicit_text: I was also involved in the Battle of Iwo Jima during World War II, another brutal and pivotal fight in the Pacific theater. The battle was fierce, and it tested every ounce of courage and strength I had. The discipline and training I gained there helped me stand up for my family and community when I returned to Columbia.

explicit_text: Adolf Hitler was one of the dictators we were fighting against in World War II. We were told that his regime represented a dark and oppressive way of life, which was why we were fighting to protect American democracy. The contrast between what we fought for abroad and the racial oppression I faced at home was stark and disheartening.

explicit_text: Isoroku Yamamoto was the Japanese admiral leading the forces we fought against in the Pacific during World War II. His strategies made battles like Guadalcanal and Iwo Jima very challenging. Knowing we were up against such formidable opponents made our victories all the more significant, though the fight back home for equality was just as tough.

explicit_text: Benito Mussolini was another fascist leader whose regime we opposed during World War II. Yet, returning home to Columbia, I saw that the fight for freedom and justice was far from over for black Americans.

explicit_text: After the incident in Columbia in 1946, the Ku Klux Klan or similar white supremacist elements began organizing to intimidate and terrorize black residents. They wanted to lynch a few black men to instill fear and maintain control. This threat forced many black people to arm themselves and stand ready to defend their community during those tense days.

They wanted to lynch a few black men to instill fear and maintain control. This threat forced many black people to arm themselves and stand ready to defend their community during those tense days.

explicit_text: During the 1946 disturbances in Columbia, the city police couldn't handle the situation, so the governor called in the National Guard and state troopers to restore order. They took over the enforcement of martial law in the black neighborhoods, where tensions were highest. From what I saw, the National Guard used some discretion and didn't escalate the violence further, which was somewhat surprising given the circumstances.

explicit_text: Eighth Street in Columbia was the heart of the black business district, just a block from the courthouse on Seventh Street. During the 1946 unrest, this one block of black-owned shops became a stronghold where black residents gathered and armed themselves to defend against white aggression. It was a place of both community and resistance during those difficult times.

explicit_text: Main Street in Columbia was where my family owned several buildings, though we rented them out to white tenants because no black business was big enough to rent such places at the time. It was just a block from the courthouse and close to the black business district on Eighth Street. During the 1946 disturbances, Main Street was a dividing line where whites would parade but rarely enter the black neighborhoods.

It marked one of the boundaries of the contested zones during that time.

explicit_text: After the 1946 incident in Columbia, the young black soldier who started the conflict fled town, and I think he might have made it as far as Nashville, Tennessee. It was part of a route he possibly took to escape the racial violence and threats from the Ku Klux Klan and others. Nashville represented a potential refuge away from the hostile environment in South Carolina.

explicit_text: I served in the United States Navy during World War II and was recently discharged when I returned to Columbia in 1946. Wearing my Navy pea coat and civilian clothes, I used my service background to navigate through tense neighborhoods during the racial disturbances. My time in the Navy gave me a sense of pride and protection, which I relied on during those difficult days back home.
